notes: Patches from WIN-013.c2 processing and WIN-010 Cycle 2 findings. Includes: code_analysis tag fixes for WIN-011 (relabels_standard) and WIN-013 (monitoring), WIN-010 detail additions for FSF argument and Kp asymmetry, WIN-010 verdict text attribution fix. For code_analysis tags, sync-code-analysis.js --apply is the preferred mechanism — the object-level patches are provided as an alternative if the script is not available.

patches.rationale: ISS-502 (major): Standard physics does NOT predict eclipse gravity anomalies. The Allais effect is unconfirmed by SG instruments (Van Camp et al. 2001). WIN-011 claims a phenomenon standard physics rejects, not one it explains. relabels_standard=true is incorrect. Confirmed by WIN-011.c2.json curmudgeon review. Preferred fix: node build-scripts/sync-code-analysis.js --apply --workspace

ISS-503 (major): WIN-013 is NOT validated anywhere in monitor.py — no domain exists for the Membach null result. Domain #7 handles WIN-012 only. WIN-014 (identical structure) correctly tags 'none'. The 'hardcoded' tag implies the dome code does something for this WIN when it does nothing. Confirmed by WIN-013.c2.json curmudgeon review. Preferred fix: node build-scripts/sync-code-analysis.js --apply --workspace

patches.find: Note: the dome model's own eclipse validation in monitor.py imposes a Kp < 2 quiet-day filter — an escape clause that voids the test whenever geomagnetic activity is elevated. This means the 'confirmation' is conditionally testable only under ideal conditions chosen by the dome itself, which further undermines the claim's robustness.
patches.replace: Note: the dome model's own eclipse validation in monitor.py imposes a Kp < 2 quiet-day filter — an escape clause that voids the test whenever geomagnetic activity is elevated. This means the 'confirmation' is conditionally testable only under ideal conditions chosen by the dome itself, which further undermines the claim's robustness. Critically, the 2017 eclipse observation underlying WIN-010 was recorded during Kp = 3–4 (geomagnetically disturbed conditions) — conditions that would void the 2026 prediction test under the model's own precondition. The model accepts a disturbed-day baseline as a full 'WIN' but imposes a quiet-day requirement for future tests, creating an asymmetric evidentiary standard. Additionally, the dome's 2026 eclipse predictions use a 'field strength factor' (FSF) for each station — claimed to derive from 'V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' — but FSF values range from 0.642 to 2.075 across stations, a 3.2× spread within just 20° of European latitude. Since H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) depends only on disc radius (not geographic latitude), dome geometry cannot produce this variation among stations at similar disc radii. The FSF spread indicates post-hoc empirical fitting to observed eclipse data, not first-principles derivation from dome parameters.
patches.rationale: ISS-504 (moderate) + ISS-505 (moderate): Adds two related Cycle 2 findings. (1) The Kp asymmetry: 2017 had Kp=3-4 (disturbed) but counted; 2026 requires Kp<2 (quiet) or voids — asymmetric standard. (2) FSF coefficients span 3.2× despite H(r) being radially symmetric — indicates empirical fitting, not dome geometry derivation. Both strengthen the non-discriminating verdict.

patches.find: Non-discriminating: Chapman's ionospheric mechanism predicts the exact signal magnitude and geometry. The dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation to produce a different prediction.
patches.replace: Non-discriminating: the Chapman ionospheric mechanism — quantitatively modeled by Meza et al. (2021, JGR Space Physics) — predicts the observed signal magnitude and geometry to within measurement uncertainty. The dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation to produce a different prediction; its FSF scaling formula uses the 2017 BOU observation as its own baseline input, making it circularly calibrated to the very event it claims to predict.
patches.rationale: ISS-506 (minor): Chapman (1933) was qualitative/semi-quantitative; Meza et al. (2021) provides the quantitative prediction that matches 2017 BOU data. The verdict text previously overclaimed precision for Chapman alone. Also adds the circularity note about FSF calibration, tying together ISS-504/505/506 into a coherent argument.

patches.find: When the crude instrument reports a marginal signal and the precise instrument finds nothing, the scientific conclusion favors the precise instrument.
patches.replace:
When the crude instrument reports a marginal signal and the precise instrument finds nothing, the scientific conclusion favors the precise instrument.

The dome additionally claims the Membach null result proves the eclipse effect is 'electromagnetic, not gravitational.' This interpretation creates a three-way contradiction: (a) if electromagnetic: spring gravimeters measure gravitational acceleration, not electromagnetic fields — a genuine EM effect should not register on a gravimeter, yet WIN-011 claims positive spring-gravimeter detection; (b) if electromagnetic: the coupling constant κ = ΔB/Δg in WIN-012 requires a non-zero gravitational anomaly (Δg) as its denominator — an electromagnetic interpretation makes Δg = 0 and κ undefined; (c) if location-dependent: the model avoids contradiction by asserting different sites respond differently, but provides no advance specification of which sites should show effects — rendering any future null result unfalsifiable. Each horn of this trilemma fatally undermines one or more other WINs in the eclipse-gravity cluster.
patches.rationale: ISS-507 (moderate): The dome's EM escape claim for WIN-013 self-destructs when examined alongside WIN-011 (positive spring-gravimeter detection) and WIN-012 (coupling constant requiring gravitational denominator). Three-way trilemma: electromagnetic interpretation invalidates WIN-011 and WIN-012; gravitational interpretation makes WIN-013 null the authoritative result; location-dependent interpretation is unfalsifiable without advance thresholds. Argument is tight enough for a patch rather than analyst expansion.
